behaviors.description: Valid JSON payloads containing markdown-heavy strings parse successfully and proceed through normal hook input handling.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The failure mode is entirely within the JSON parsing function — does it correctly handle backticks, code fences, and `$vars` inside JSON string values? No module boundary is crossed; this is pure parse-function logic. REAL-INFRA: No OS, network, or subprocess is needed; an in-process call with a crafted string reproduces the failure. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock — the parser either accepts the input or it doesn't. LLM-DEP: No AI/ML involved. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but rejected because the behavior's failure mode is entirely within the parser's acceptance of the input, before any module handoff occurs.
behaviors.test_description: Call the hook input parser directly with a JSON string containing markdown elements (triple backticks, inline code, `$var` tokens, nested quotes). Assert that parsing succeeds, the resulting object matches the expected structure, and no error branch is taken.

behaviors.description: Truly malformed JSON payloads produce a `json_parse_failed` trace record with enough context to diagnose the malformed section.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior asserts that the parser and the trace sink cooperate — the error path must invoke the trace module and supply it with contextual information about the failure site. Checking this at Unit by mocking the trace sink would hide whether the right context fields are actually passed and recorded. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network needed; an in-process trace sink can capture the record. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking the trace sink would make any call to it pass, hiding whether the record contains 'enough context to diagnose.' Wiring the real (or test-real) trace module is required. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: System was considered but rejected because the trace sink is in-process and no OS/subprocess behavior is under test here.
behaviors.test_description: Wire the parser and the real trace sink together in a test harness. Feed a malformed JSON string (e.g., trailing junk, unclosed bracket). Assert that (a) a `json_parse_failed` trace event is emitted, and (b) the event payload includes the malformed section or offset so the failure can be diagnosed without additional tooling.

behaviors.description: The local error-policy path maps parse failure to a handled result (no throw) and marks telemetry intent.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior is about the error-policy function's own logic: catch the exception, return a handled result, set a telemetry-intent flag. This is a single control-flow path in one function. REAL-INFRA: No external infrastructure. MOCK-HIDE: The telemetry intent is a field/flag on the returned result object — asserting it directly without any real sink is sufficient. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered for the telemetry part but rejected because 'marks telemetry intent' describes writing a flag onto the result, not invoking a separate telemetry module; the assertion is on the returned value, not on downstream emission.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the error-policy function with a simulated parse-failure exception. Assert that (a) no exception propagates, (b) the returned result is in the handled/error variant, and (c) the telemetry-intent field on the result is set to the expected value.

behaviors.description: In real process execution, malformed stdin does not crash the hook process and still emits the expected trace signal before exit.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The behavior explicitly names 'real process execution.' Process-level crash (unhandled exception, non-zero exit, SIGABRT) vs. graceful shutdown is only observable through real subprocess behavior. REAL-INFRA: Yes — 'real process execution' and 'exit' unambiguously require a real subprocess. An in-process function call cannot show whether the process crashes or exits gracefully, nor can it observe OS-level signal emission. MOCK-HIDE: Any in-process mock hides the crash/exit distinction entirely. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but the behavior's own text 'real process execution … before exit' disqualifies it — process exit is an OS boundary that only a real subprocess can expose.
behaviors.test_description: Launch the hook binary as a subprocess with malformed JSON on stdin. Assert that (a) the process exits with a non-crash exit code, and (b) stdout/stderr or the trace output file contains the expected `json_parse_failed` signal before the process terminates.

behaviors.description: A real hook invocation path (stdin -> parser -> trace sink) produces the expected trace artifact when malformed input is supplied.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The parenthetical description '(stdin -> parser -> trace sink)' and the term 'trace artifact' indicate end-to-end execution through all three stages. REAL-INFRA: 'stdin' in context of a hook invocation implies a real process boundary — stdin is a pipe to a running process. A 'trace artifact' (file, structured output) only materializes through real I/O. In-process wiring cannot reproduce the stdin pipe or file-system artifact production. MOCK-HIDE: Mocking any link in the chain hides whether the full path from stdin to artifact is intact. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered but 'real hook invocation path … trace artifact' requires real process stdin and real file/output artifact — both are OS-level I/O boundaries that integration-level in-process fakes cannot replicate faithfully.
behaviors.test_description: Invoke the hook via its real entry point (subprocess or equivalent OS execution), feeding malformed JSON to stdin via a pipe. After the hook exits, read the trace artifact from the expected output path and assert it contains a `json_parse_failed` record with the appropriate fields.

behaviors.description: Regression tests distinguish malformed-JSON failures from valid-markdown payloads, preventing false attribution to markdown content itself.
behaviors.justification: MOCK-MISS: The discriminating logic lives entirely in the parser: does it reject truly malformed JSON while accepting valid JSON that happens to contain markdown? No module boundary is crossed; the test needs two inputs and two asserted outcomes from one function. REAL-INFRA: No OS/network. MOCK-HIDE: Nothing to mock — both inputs go straight to the parser. LLM-DEP: No. MULTI-STEP: No. REJECTION-GATE: Integration was considered because the issue description mentions prior confusion, but 'distinguish malformed-JSON failures from valid-markdown payloads' is a property of the parse function's acceptance criteria, not of a multi-module interaction — a single parametrized unit test covers both cases definitively.
behaviors.test_description: Parametrize the parser with (a) valid JSON containing heavy markdown content and (b) truly malformed JSON (trailing junk, invalid syntax). Assert that (a) succeeds without error and (b) triggers the `json_parse_failed` error path. Use the same assertion layer for both to make the distinction explicit and prevent future conflation.
